source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure API Keys Are Restricted To Use by Only Specified Hosts and Apps

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure API Keys Are Restricted To Use by Only Specified Hosts and Apps
input_row.description: API Keys should only be used for services in cases where other authentication methods are unavailable. In this case, unrestricted keys are insecure because they can be viewed publicly, such as from within a browser, or they can be accessed on a device where the key resides. It is recommended to restrict API key usage to trusted hosts, HTTP referrers and apps. It is recommended to use the more secure standard authentication flow instead.
input_row.rationale: Security risks involved in using API-Keys appear below: • API keys are simple encrypted strings • API keys do not identify the user or the application making the API request • API keys are typically accessible to clients, making it easy to discover and steal an API key In light of these potential risks, Google recommends using the standard authentication flow instead of API keys. However, there are limited cases where API keys are more appropriate. For example, if there is a mobile application that needs to use the Google Cloud Translation API, but doesn't otherwise need a backend server, API keys are the simplest way to authenticate to that API. In order to reduce attack vectors, API-Keys can be restricted only to trusted hosts, HTTP referrers and applications. Impact: Setting Application Restrictions may break existing application functioning, if not done carefully.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to APIs & Services\Credentials using https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials 2. In the section API Keys, Click the API Key Name. The API Key properties display on a new page. 3. For every API Key, ensure the section Key restrictions parameter Application restrictions is not set to None. Or, 1. Ensure Application restrictions is set to HTTP referrers and the referrer is not set to wild-cards (* or *.[TLD] or *.[TLD]/*) allowing access to any/wide HTTP referrer(s) Or, 1. Ensure Application restrictions is set to IP addresses and referrer is not set to any host (0.0.0.0 or 0.0.0.0/0 or ::0) From Google Cloud Command Line 1. Run the following from within the project you wish to audit gcloud services api-keys list --filter="-restrictions:*" -- format="table[box](displayName:label='Key With No Restrictions')
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console Leaving Keys in Place 1. Go to APIs & Services\Credentials using https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials 2. In the section API Keys, Click the API Key Name. The API Key properties display on a new page. 3. In the Key restrictions section, set the application restrictions to any of HTTP referrers, IP addresses, Android apps, iOS apps. 4. Click Save. 5. Repeat steps 2,3,4 for every unrestricted API key. Note: Do not set HTTP referrers to wild-cards (* or *.[TLD] or .[TLD]/ ) allowing access to any/wide HTTP referrer(s) Do not set IP addresses and referrer to any host (0.0.0.0 or 0.0.0.0/0 or ::0) Removing Keys Another option is to remove the keys entirely. 1. Go to APIs & Services\Credentials using https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials 2. In the section API Keys, select the checkbox next to each key you wish to remove 3. Select Delete and confirm. Default Value: By default, Application Restrictions are set to None. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/api-keys 2. https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/services/api-keys/list 3. https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/services/api-keys/update
input_row.impact: Setting Application Restrictions may break existing application functioning, if not done carefully.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

The control checks if API keys in GCP are restricted to specific hosts, HTTP referrers, or applications. This is a property that can be queried and verified using the GCP API services, particularly with the `gcloud` command-line tool. The audit steps provided include specific `gcloud` commands to check for API keys without restrictions, indicating that this control can be automated.

- ✓ Can GCP APIs query this property? → Yes, through GCP APIs and `gcloud` commands.
- ✓ Is this a standard GCP service property (encryption, logging, access control, network rules)? → Yes, it involves access control of API keys.
- ✓ Does Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) SDK have methods to check this? → Yes, by using `gcloud services api-keys list`.
- ✓ Does GCP CLI have commands to retrieve this? → Yes, through the `gcloud` command mentioned in the audit instructions.
- ✓ Does audit mention Google Cloud SDK (gcloud)/GCP CLI commands? → Yes, it explicitly mentions `gcloud services api-keys list` for checking API key restrictions.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_services_api_keys_restrictions_enforced

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

- **GCP API Call or Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) SDK Methods to Use:** The `gcloud` command mentioned in the audit instructions: `gcloud services api-keys list --filter="-restrictions:*" --format="table[box](displayName:label='Key With No Restrictions')"` is used to list all API keys that do not have any restrictions.
  
- **Specific Resources to Enumerate:** Enumerate all API keys within a GCP project.
  
- **Exact Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:** Verify that each API key has application restrictions set to either HTTP referrers, IP addresses, Android apps, or iOS apps, and not set to wild-cards or any host which would allow unrestricted access.
  
- **Clear Pass/Fail Criteria with Expected Values:**
  - **Pass:** API keys are restricted to specific HTTP referrers, IP addresses, or mobile apps and do not use wildcards or unrestricted settings.
  - **Fail:** API keys have no application restrictions or misuse wildcards, leaving them unrestricted. 

By using the provided `gcloud` commands and further verification of key restrictions through the Google Cloud SDK or API, this control can be fully automated and does not require manual inspection.
step2_review:
---

### 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated classification:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is correct. GCP API keys and their restrictions are fully manageable and queryable via GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud`). No manual inspection is required unless there are edge cases or custom implementations outside standard GCP API key management.

- **GCP SDK/CLI Capabilities:**  
  The `gcloud services api-keys list` command is valid and can enumerate API keys and their restriction properties. The API Keys API (`apikeys.googleapis.com`) also supports listing and describing keys and their restrictions programmatically.

- **Other Automation Tools:**  
  While GCP Config (Config Connector) and IaC tools like Terraform can manage API keys, the audit is focused on detection, not provisioning, so `gcloud` and direct API calls are appropriate.

---

### 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **Score: 8/10**

- **Automation Details:**  
  - The command `gcloud services api-keys list --filter="-restrictions:*" --format="table[box](displayName:label='Key With No Restrictions')"` is correct for listing API keys with no restrictions.
  - However, this only identifies keys with *no* restrictions. It does **not** catch keys with overly broad/wildcard restrictions (e.g., HTTP referrer set to `*` or IP range `0.0.0.0/0`), which are also non-compliant per the control intent.
  - The audit does not mention using `gcloud services api-keys describe <KEY_ID>` or equivalent API calls to inspect the details of each key's restrictions for wildcards or overly broad settings.

- **Program Name:**  
  - `gcp_services_api_keys_restrictions_enforced` is descriptive, but could be more specific (see Improvements).

- **Manual Steps:**  
  - Not applicable, as the approach is automated.

---

### 3. IMPROVEMENTS

- **Missing Considerations:**
  - The current approach only flags keys with *no* restrictions. It should also flag keys with restrictions that are too broad (e.g., HTTP referrer set to `*`, IP restriction set to `0.0.0.0/0`, or mobile app restrictions with wildcards).
  - The audit should include a step to iterate through each API key and inspect the actual values of the restriction fields, using either:
    - `gcloud services api-keys describe <KEY_ID>`
    - Or the [API Keys API](https://cloud.google.com/api-keys/docs/reference/rest/v2/projects.locations.keys/get) directly.

- **Alternative GCP API Methods:**
  - Use the REST API: `GET https://apikeys.googleapis.com/v2/projects/{project}/locations/global/keys/{key_id}` to programmatically inspect restriction details.
  - Consider using [Cloud Asset Inventory](https://cloud.google.com/asset-inventory/docs/overview) for organization-wide API key enumeration and restriction checks.

- **Better Program Name Suggestion:**
  - `gcp_services_api_keys_application_restrictions_enforced`
  - Or, more specifically: `gcp_services_api_keys_no_unrestricted_or_wildcard_restrictions`

---

### 4. CONFIDENCE: **MEDIUM**

- The assessment is mostly correct, but the omission of wildcard/broad restriction detection is a significant gap for a security/compliance audit. The approach is fundamentally sound, but incomplete for full CIS compliance.

---

### 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **Modify**

- **Modify** the automation to:
  - Not only list keys with no restrictions, but also inspect all restriction fields for wildcards or overly broad values.
  - Update documentation to clarify that both "no restrictions" and "wildcard/broad restrictions" are non-compliant.
  - Update the program name to reflect the specificity of the check.

---

#### **Summary Table**

| Section             | Verdict/Score | Notes                                                                                  |
|---------------------|--------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Approach Validation | AGREE         | Automated is correct; GCP APIs and gcloud fully support this.                          |
| Technical Accuracy  | 8/10          | Accurate for "no restrictions", but misses wildcards/overly broad restrictions.        |
| Improvements        | See above     | Must check for wildcards; improve program name; consider Cloud Asset Inventory.        |
| Confidence          | MEDIUM        | Approach is solid, but incomplete for full compliance.                                 |
| Final Recommendation| MODIFY        | Expand automation to detect wildcards; clarify documentation; update program name.     |

---

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identify that GCP API key restrictions are fully queryable and auditable via the Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud`) and GCP APIs. The audit can be automated by listing all API keys and programmatically checking their restriction properties. However, to fully meet the control intent, automation must not only detect unrestricted keys but also keys with overly broad (wildcard) restrictions, which requires parsing the restriction details for insecure patterns. This is achievable with available GCP APIs and CLI tools.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
gcp_services_api_keys_application_restrictions_enforced

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **GCP Resource/Service:** API Keys (API Gateway/Cloud APIs)
- **API/CLI Method:**  
  - List all API keys: `gcloud services api-keys list` or via the API Keys API (`apikeys.googleapis.com/v2/projects.locations.keys.list`)
  - For each key, describe details: `gcloud services api-keys describe <KEY_ID>` or `apikeys.googleapis.com/v2/projects.locations.keys.get`
- **Properties to Check:**  
  - `restrictions` field, specifically `apiTargets`, `browserKeyRestrictions`, `serverKeyRestrictions`, `androidKeyRestrictions`, `iosKeyRestrictions`
  - For `browserKeyRestrictions`, check `allowedReferrers` for wildcards (`*`, `*.[TLD]`, `*.[TLD]/*`)
  - For `serverKeyRestrictions`, check `allowedIps` for `0.0.0.0/0`
  - For all keys, ensure `restrictions` is not empty and does not contain insecure patterns
- **Pass Criteria:**  
  - Every API key has `restrictions` set
  - No API key allows wildcards or overly broad patterns in referrers or IPs
- **Fail Criteria:**  
  - Any API key has `restrictions` set to `None`/empty
  - Any API key allows wildcards (`*`, `*.[TLD]`, etc.) or `0.0.0.0/0` in restrictions

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
Both AI opinions agreed on automation, but the second opinion correctly noted that the automation must check for both unrestricted and overly broad (wildcard) restrictions. My decision incorporates this nuance for full compliance.

**SOURCE:** CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
